$MELI anunció el lanzamiento de Point Tap, su propia solución de ‘Tap to phone’ que permite a pequeños negocios, emprendedores y profesionales independientes aceptar pagos con tarjeta directamente en su celular a través de tecnología NFC.

La herramienta, que se desplegará progresivamente durante el mes de marzo, convierte smartphones a partir de Android 10 en una terminal de cobro sin contacto.

Con Point Tap, los comercios podrán ingresar el monto desde su celular y recibir el pago simplemente acercando una tarjeta con tecnología contactless. 

“Buscamos que más emprendedores puedan aceptar pagos digitales desde un dispositivo que  ya forma parte de su operación diaria, como es el celular.

Esto derriba más barreras a la digitalización y permite a pequeños comerciantes profesionalizarse de manera práctica y segura”, 
 Enrique Horcasitas, director senior de Pymes de Mercado Pago México.

I read the transcript for an hour long interview that just dropped with $MELI's Fernando Yunes, President of MELI Brazil. Here are his main points.

He revealed some alpha for the company’s trajectory in 2026 and beyond. Their logistics network is ruthless. MELI now delivers 50% of all purchases in Brazil on the same day or the next day. Nearly 80% of packages arrive within two days. They achieve this using a dedicated fleet of 9 aircraft flying full-time. They also have over 1000 developers working exclusively on logistics algorithms. Last year alone, they delivered 3.3B products.

They are also scaling automation. MELI deployed 360 shelf-moving robots in their distribution centers. These robots bring the inventory directly to the workers. This change reduced product preparation time by 27%. It also allowed the company to hire 10,000 more workers for higher-value tasks instead of replacing human labor.

MELI is aggressively buying market share. Brazil makes up over 50% of their total revenue. To crush low-cost competitors like Shopee and Temu, MELI slashed its free-shipping threshold last year. This is a critical margin investment. It locks in customer loyalty and massively increases purchase frequency. MELI has now doubled its Brazilian market share from roughly 20% in 2020 to over 40% today.

The real profit engine is outside the core marketplace. Mercado Pago is a fintech giant. The volume of transactions processed by Mercado Pago outside of the Mercado Libre platform is four times larger than the marketplace itself. Mercado Ads is also booming. It is now the third-largest online advertising platform in Latin America. MELI is using its massive consumer data to help brands target shoppers across the entire internet.

They are unlocking massive new markets for the future. Ecom penetration in Brazil is still only ~16%-17%. MELI expects this to cross 20% in the next three years. They are also building a robust B2B platform. They recently bought a physical pharmacy in São Paulo. They did this strictly to secure regulatory licenses and learn the 1P model. Their goal is to launch a massive 3P pharmaceutical marketplace in Brazil.

MELI is operating completely independently of Brazil’s high interest rates and macro struggles. They are trying to formalize the economy. Over the last two years, their platform directly facilitated the creation of 250k newly registered businesses.

The growth in Latin America is much more durable because:

1) E-commerce penetration is low
2) Financial access is low
3) Economies are growing faster than developed markets

E-commerce penetration in many South American countries remains significantly lower than in developed markets, leaving substantial room for online retail expansion.

Furthermore, contrary to popular belief, at maturity. e-commerce penetration in these markets will be even higher than in developed markets, because 1) Lower income people tend to price match more 2) there are fewer convenient brick and mortar commerce options. You don't have as many tire shops in 20K town in Brazil as you do in the US. So a Brazilian living there would rather order online than drive 1H to the nearest large tire shop to get the specific tires he needs.

Moreover, a large portion of the population in South America remains underbanked or unbanked, limiting access to traditional financial services. Mercado Libre addresses this gap through Mercado Pago, which provides digital payments, credit, and financial tools. $AMZN doesn't have a fintech arm to drive business on its platform. So they need to spend more on CAC and have lower prices to attract consumers.

Lastly, South American economies are experiencing faster long-term growth rates than mature developed markets, driven by urbanization, rising middle classes, and increasing digital adoption. This economic expansion creates a favorable environment for Mercado Libre to scale its e-commerce and fintech ecosystems.
